title: Si-Te-Cah Cave Remains
summary: Lovelock Cave (Nevada) yielded remains and artifacts linked in Paiute tradition to red-haired giants.
detail: Excavations in the early twentieth century recovered basketry, duck decoys, and human remains from Lovelock Cave. Paiute oral history speaks of the Si-Te-Cah - red-haired cannibal giants driven into the cave and smoked out. Popular accounts inflate stature claims far beyond what osteological study supports. The cultural tradition is real; the giant-sized skeleton narrative remains unverified and often distorted in secondary retellings.

title: The Cardiff Giant
summary: A carved gypsum figure buried and 'discovered' in 1869 - one of America's most famous archaeological frauds.
detail: George Hull commissioned a ten-foot gypsum figure, aged it with acids, buried it on a farm near Cardiff, New York, and arranged its 'discovery.' Crowds paid to see the petrified giant. P.T. Barnum later made a copy when denied purchase of the original. Hull confessed; the figure survives in museum collections as a monument to credulity rather than antiquity.

title: Giant of Kandahar Reports
summary: Circulating oral accounts of a red-haired giant engaged by special forces near Kandahar. No official corroboration.
detail: Reports claim… that coalition special operations personnel encountered a towering red-haired figure in the mountains near Kandahar in the early 2000s. According to the circulating account, the being carried a spear, wore rudimentary armor, and was killed after an engagement. No official records corroborate the story. The account spread through podcasts, forums, and second-hand military storytelling. Treat as unverified oral legend, not documented history.

title: Giant of Kunar Reports
summary: Similar modern military legend from eastern Afghanistan. Unverified; presented here only as circulating folklore.
detail: According to the circulating account, personnel in Kunar Province reported a massive figure observed or engaged in remote terrain. Reports claim… extreme stature and unusual physical traits. No official records corroborate these accounts. Grouped with other twenty-first-century giant legends that migrate through online communities and veteran storytelling networks.

title: Nephilim Skeleton Claims
summary: Recurring internet and print claims of giant biblical skeletons - frequently photo-manipulated or unsubstantiated.
detail: From nineteenth-century newspaper wonders to modern viral images, claims of Nephilim bones surface repeatedly. Many photographs have been demonstrated as composites or museum props. No peer-reviewed osteological series establishes a population of multi-meter humans corresponding to these stories. The biblical and Second Temple literary tradition is ancient and significant; physical 'giant skeleton' claims remain unverified.

title: Patagonian Giants of Early Exploration
summary: European explorers described towering indigenous people of Patagonia - later understood as exaggeration and cultural misperception.
detail: Magellan's chroniclers and later voyagers wrote of men so tall Europeans reached only to their waists. Actual Tehuelche stature was often above European averages of the era, but not the legendary heights recorded. The 'Patagonian giant' entered European popular geography as fact for centuries before being scaled down by better ethnography.

title: Southwestern 'Giant' Burial Claims
summary: Sensational claims of giant burials in the American Southwest, often recycled without primary excavation records.
detail: Nineteenth- and early twentieth-century newspapers occasionally reported giant skeletons from cliff dwellings and mounds. Most lack museum catalogue numbers, measured osteology, or verifiable provenience. Some are known fabrications; others are garbled accounts of ordinary remains. Modern archaeology of Ancestral Puebloan sites does not support a giant population.

title: Og's Iron Bed (Deuteronomy)
summary: Biblical text describes Og of Bashan's enormous iron bed - literary/mythic scale, not excavated furniture.
detail: Deuteronomy records Og's bedstead of iron, nine cubits by four. The passage functions within ancient Near Eastern royal hyperbole and tradition about the last of the Rephaim. No archaeological bed matching the description has been recovered; the text remains a literary witness to ancient giant tradition rather than a measured artifact.
